title: Tuberculosis — Latent
oneLiner: Latent TB infection (LTBI) — asymptomatic, non-infectious, positive IGRA/TST; treatment prevents reactivation (5–10% lifetime risk, higher in immunocompromised).

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Active pulmonary TB (undiagnosed) – the real killer:** if you assume “latent” but they actually have active disease, a single-drug LTBI regimen will induce resistance and allow progression to cavitary disease, haemoptysis, respiratory failure, or miliary spread.  
  *Red flag finding:* **any respiratory symptom (cough > 3 weeks, haemoptysis) OR abnormal CXR (apical infiltrates, cavities)** — stop, reclassify as active, start 4-drug therapy.
- **TB meningitis (rare but catastrophic) – presents with subacute headache, fever, cranial nerve palsies.**  
  *Red flag finding:* **fever + headache + neck stiffness + CSF lymphocytosis** – this is a neurological emergency; start empiric anti-TB therapy immediately without waiting for culture.
sections.Presentation:
- **No symptoms** – by definition, LTBI is asymptomatic. If they have any symptom (cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss, haemoptysis), it’s active TB until proven otherwise.
- **Incidental finding** – positive IGRA (QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus or T-SPOT.TB) or positive Mantoux test (≥5 mm, ≥10 mm, or ≥15 mm depending on risk group; see UK guidelines).
- **Key history** – TB contact (household, prison, homeless shelter), recent immigration from high-prevalence country, immunosuppression (HIV, anti-TNF therapy, transplant, dialysis, gastrectomy, silicosis).
- **Exam findings** – normal. No crackles, no lymphadenopathy. Any abnormal chest signs suggest active disease.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Latent TB (LTBI)** – positive IGRA/TST, no symptoms, normal CXR.
- **Active pulmonary TB** – positive IGRA/TST PLUS symptoms ± abnormal CXR. **Splitter:** persistent cough > 3 weeks or haemoptysis = active TB; absence = LTBI.
- **Active extrapulmonary TB** – lymph node, pleural, bone/joint, meningeal, etc. – focal signs (e.g. painless lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion, spinal tenderness) differentiate from LTBI.
- **Non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM)** – e.g. *M. avium* complex – can cross-react with IGRA/TST; culture or molecular testing needed. Risk factors: COPD, bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis.
- **History of BCG vaccination** – may cause false-positive TST (less so with IGRA). Check BCG scar and recent travel/contact.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** none. LTBI is a laboratory diagnosis after active TB is excluded.
- **Bloods:**  
  - IGRA (QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus or T-SPOT.TB) – first-line screening.  
  - Baseline LFTs (before starting LTBI treatment).  
  - FBC, U&E, LFT – annually or as baseline.  
  - HIV serology – mandatory in new TB diagnoses (latent or active).  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** PA chest X-ray. If normal, LTBI likely. If any abnormality (apical scar, hilar lymphadenopathy, fibrotic changes), refer for sputum cultures to rule out active TB.  
  - **Second-line (if CXR equivocal):** CT chest.  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Sputum for smear, culture, and molecular (GeneXpert) – if any CXR abnormality or symptoms.  
  - Bronchoscopy (rarely needed) – if sputum-negative but high suspicion of active TB.  
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Positive IGRA (or TST ≥ 5 mm in high-risk groups, ≥ 10 mm in medium risk, ≥ 15 mm in low risk per BTS/NICE)  
  - **AND** no clinical or radiographic evidence of active TB  
  - **AND** no prior treatment for TB  
- **Never-miss test:** **Chest X-ray** – a normal CXR is the single most important test to separate LTBI from active TB. Omitting it risks treating active disease with monotherapy.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Usually not an ED presentation. If LTBI incidentally identified (e.g. positive IGRA on admission for another reason), do **not start treatment** until active TB excluded and patient stable.  
  - If any red flag (cough, fever, CXR abnormality) → isolate patient, start 4-drug therapy (Rifampicin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) and refer TB team.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Ensure CXR reviewed by radiologist.  
  - Check baseline LFTs.  
  - Check HIV status.  
  - Refer to TB specialist nurse/clinic for treatment decision.  
  - Educate patient: LTBI is not infectious; treatment is to prevent future disease.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Decide on indication for treatment (most LTBI patients are offered treatment, except very low-risk if no contacts – see NICE).  
  - Choice of regimen (adult):  
    - 6 months isoniazid 300 mg + pyridoxine 10–25 mg daily  
    - OR 3 months isoniazid + rifampicin (Rifinah 300/150 mg; weight-based)  
    - OR 4 months rifampicin alone (less evidence, used if INH contraindicated)  
  - Monitor LFTs monthly during treatment (more frequently if pre-existing liver disease or symptoms).  
  - Document completion of treatment (check adherence, end-of-treatment note).  
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reasons):**  
  - No specific default orders. If started on LTBI therapy while inpatient:  
    - Diet as normal  
    - VTE prophylaxis per local policy (no interaction)  
    - Observations once daily (if on rifampicin, monitor for drug-induced hepatitis)  
    - Flag for drug interaction review (rifampicin induces many drugs – warfarin, OCP, antiretrovirals, corticosteroids, etc.)
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **First-line agents (adult doses, oral):**  
  - Isoniazid 300 mg once daily (5 mg/kg; max 300 mg)  
    - **Always co-prescribe pyridoxine 10–25 mg daily** to prevent peripheral neuropathy.  
  - Rifampicin 600 mg once daily (10 mg/kg; max 600 mg)  
    - *If using combination tablet: Rifinah 300 mg (INH) / 150 mg (Rif) – 2 tablets daily for weight ≥ 50 kg; otherwise check BNF.*  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Rifampicin monotherapy 600 mg daily for 4 months (if INH contraindicated).  
  - Rifapentine + isoniazid weekly for 3 months (not first-line in UK; used under specialist guidance).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Rifampicin: strong CYP450 inducer → avoid with warfarin, oral contraceptives (use barrier), HIV protease inhibitors, many others. Check full drug interaction list.  
  - Isoniazid: avoid alcohol (increases hepatotoxicity), monitor LFTs; caution in pre-existing liver disease.  
  - Both: pregnancy – isoniazid + rifampicin can be used (with pyridoxine); rifampicin safe in breastfeeding.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Isoniazid: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; hepatotoxicity monitoring essential.  
  - Rifampicin: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (check BNF).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Prescribing isoniazid alone in active TB** – leads to resistance and treatment failure.  
  - **Forgetting pyridoxine** with isoniazid – preventable peripheral neuropathy (especially in malnourished, diabetics, alcoholics, pregnancy).  
  - **Starting rifampicin without checking concurrent medications** – catastrophic drug interactions (e.g. sub-therapeutic warfarin → stroke; reduced OCP efficacy → pregnancy; undetectable antiretroviral levels → HIV treatment failure).
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**  
  - Drug-induced hepatotoxicity (INH, rifampicin) – LFT elevation, hepatitis (symptoms: nausea, vomiting, jaundice, dark urine). Most common reason for discontinuation. NICE: stop if ALT > 3x ULN with symptoms or > 5x ULN without symptoms.  
  - Hypersensitivity reactions (rash, fever) – both INH and rifampicin.  
  - Thrombocytopenia (rifampicin) – rare; check FBC if bruising/bleeding.  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Peripheral neuropathy (INH) – preventable with pyridoxine.  
  - Optic neuritis (ethambutol – not used in LTBI, but if misclassified).  
  - Iron deficiency (rifampicin – mild, rarely significant).
sections.Prognosis:
- Excellent if treatment completed – reduces risk of reactivation by 60–90% (depending on regimen, adherence, and underlying immune status).  
- Without treatment, 5–10% of immunocompetent patients develop active TB over a lifetime (highest risk in first 2 years).  
- In HIV co-infection, risk of reactivation is 10% per year.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always document “LTBI” vs “active TB” clearly in notes and on the drug chart** – this avoids dangerous monotherapy.  
- **Give pyridoxine with isoniazid** – write “pyridoxine 10 mg daily” as a standing prescription; educate patient why.  
- **Perform a baseline CXR and LFTs before starting LTBI treatment** – and repeat LFTs monthly (or at 2 weeks if risk factors).  
- **Check for drug interactions every time a new drug is added** – rifampicin is a common cause of “unexplained” INR rise or OCP failure.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Do not treat LTBI without ruling out active TB** – monotherapy will breed resistance and can be fatal.  
- **Do not start LTBI treatment without baseline LFTs** – you cannot attribute hepatotoxicity to drugs if you don’t have a baseline.  
- **Do not assume IGRA positive = LTBI** – false positives occur with NTM, recent BCG (less so), lab error. Always correlate with risk and CXR.  
- **Do not forget to check HIV status** – LTBI treatment is especially important in HIV, but drug interactions with antiretrovirals (especially rifampicin) must be managed.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**  
  - New onset fever, cough, night sweats, weight loss – may indicate active TB (or treatment failure).  
  - Symptoms of hepatitis: jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, unexplained fatigue.  
  - Numbness, tingling, or burning in hands/feet (INH neuropathy) – stop isoniazid, start high-dose pyridoxine 50 mg TDS, and inform TB team.  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - TB clinic review at 1 month, then monthly while on treatment (for LFTs, adherence check, side effects).  
  - After completion: discharge unless ongoing risk (e.g. HIV) – then annual review.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**  
  - LFTs ≥ 3x ULN with symptoms, or ≥ 5x ULN without symptoms – stop drugs, discuss with hepatology/TB team.  
  - Suspected drug-induced rash, urticaria, or anaphylaxis.  
  - Patient develops symptoms of active TB while on LTBI treatment – stop therapy, start 4-drug regimen, isolate.  
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**  
  - Acute liver failure (jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy) – rare but critical.  
  - Anaphylaxis to rifampicin (hypotension, angioedema) – treat with adrenaline, stop drug.  
- **Use NEWS2 thresholds** – LTBI patients should have NEWS2 ≤ 2; if rising, investigate for intercurrent illness or drug reaction.

rawMarkdown:
## Tuberculosis — Latent

**One-liner:** Latent TB infection (LTBI) — asymptomatic, non-infectious, positive IGRA/TST; treatment prevents reactivation (5–10% lifetime risk, higher in immunocompromised).

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?

- **Active pulmonary TB (undiagnosed) – the real killer:** if you assume “latent” but they actually have active disease, a single-drug LTBI regimen will induce resistance and allow progression to cavitary disease, haemoptysis, respiratory failure, or miliary spread.  
  *Red flag finding:* **any respiratory symptom (cough > 3 weeks, haemoptysis) OR abnormal CXR (apical infiltrates, cavities)** — stop, reclassify as active, start 4-drug therapy.
- **TB meningitis (rare but catastrophic) – presents with subacute headache, fever, cranial nerve palsies.**  
  *Red flag finding:* **fever + headache + neck stiffness + CSF lymphocytosis** – this is a neurological emergency; start empiric anti-TB therapy immediately without waiting for culture.

### Presentation

- **No symptoms** – by definition, LTBI is asymptomatic. If they have any symptom (cough, fever, night sweats, weight loss, haemoptysis), it’s active TB until proven otherwise.
- **Incidental finding** – positive IGRA (QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus or T-SPOT.TB) or positive Mantoux test (≥5 mm, ≥10 mm, or ≥15 mm depending on risk group; see UK guidelines).
- **Key history** – TB contact (household, prison, homeless shelter), recent immigration from high-prevalence country, immunosuppression (HIV, anti-TNF therapy, transplant, dialysis, gastrectomy, silicosis).
- **Exam findings** – normal. No crackles, no lymphadenopathy. Any abnormal chest signs suggest active disease.

### Differential Diagnosis

- **Latent TB (LTBI)** – positive IGRA/TST, no symptoms, normal CXR.
- **Active pulmonary TB** – positive IGRA/TST PLUS symptoms ± abnormal CXR. **Splitter:** persistent cough > 3 weeks or haemoptysis = active TB; absence = LTBI.
- **Active extrapulmonary TB** – lymph node, pleural, bone/joint, meningeal, etc. – focal signs (e.g. painless lymphadenopathy, pleural effusion, spinal tenderness) differentiate from LTBI.
- **Non-tuberculous mycobacteria (NTM)** – e.g. *M. avium* complex – can cross-react with IGRA/TST; culture or molecular testing needed. Risk factors: COPD, bronchiectasis, cystic fibrosis.
- **History of BCG vaccination** – may cause false-positive TST (less so with IGRA). Check BCG scar and recent travel/contact.

### Investigations

- **Bedside:** none. LTBI is a laboratory diagnosis after active TB is excluded.
- **Bloods:**  
  - IGRA (QuantiFERON-TB Gold Plus or T-SPOT.TB) – first-line screening.  
  - Baseline LFTs (before starting LTBI treatment).  
  - FBC, U&E, LFT – annually or as baseline.  
  - HIV serology – mandatory in new TB diagnoses (latent or active).  
- **Imaging:**  
  - **First-line:** PA chest X-ray. If normal, LTBI likely. If any abnormality (apical scar, hilar lymphadenopathy, fibrotic changes), refer for sputum cultures to rule out active TB.  
  - **Second-line (if CXR equivocal):** CT chest.  
- **Special tests:**  
  - Sputum for smear, culture, and molecular (GeneXpert) – if any CXR abnormality or symptoms.  
  - Bronchoscopy (rarely needed) – if sputum-negative but high suspicion of active TB.  
- **Diagnostic criteria:**  
  - Positive IGRA (or TST ≥ 5 mm in high-risk groups, ≥ 10 mm in medium risk, ≥ 15 mm in low risk per BTS/NICE)  
  - **AND** no clinical or radiographic evidence of active TB  
  - **AND** no prior treatment for TB  
- **Never-miss test:** **Chest X-ray** – a normal CXR is the single most important test to separate LTBI from active TB. Omitting it risks treating active disease with monotherapy.

### Management

- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**  
  - Usually not an ED presentation. If LTBI incidentally identified (e.g. positive IGRA on admission for another reason), do **not start treatment** until active TB excluded and patient stable.  
  - If any red flag (cough, fever, CXR abnormality) → isolate patient, start 4-drug therapy (Rifampicin, Isoniazid, Pyrazinamide, Ethambutol) and refer TB team.  
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**  
  - Ensure CXR reviewed by radiologist.  
  - Check baseline LFTs.  
  - Check HIV status.  
  - Refer to TB specialist nurse/clinic for treatment decision.  
  - Educate patient: LTBI is not infectious; treatment is to prevent future disease.  
- **Long-term / outpatient:**  
  - Decide on indication for treatment (most LTBI patients are offered treatment, except very low-risk if no contacts – see NICE).  
  - Choice of regimen (adult):  
    - 6 months isoniazid 300 mg + pyridoxine 10–25 mg daily  
    - OR 3 months isoniazid + rifampicin (Rifinah 300/150 mg; weight-based)  
    - OR 4 months rifampicin alone (less evidence, used if INH contraindicated)  
  - Monitor LFTs monthly during treatment (more frequently if pre-existing liver disease or symptoms).  
  - Document completion of treatment (check adherence, end-of-treatment note).  
- **Default Orders (if admitted for other reasons):**  
  - No specific default orders. If started on LTBI therapy while inpatient:  
    - Diet as normal  
    - VTE prophylaxis per local policy (no interaction)  
    - Observations once daily (if on rifampicin, monitor for drug-induced hepatitis)  
    - Flag for drug interaction review (rifampicin induces many drugs – warfarin, OCP, antiretrovirals, corticosteroids, etc.)

### Drugs & Doses

- **First-line agents (adult doses, oral):**  
  - Isoniazid 300 mg once daily (5 mg/kg; max 300 mg)  
    - **Always co-prescribe pyridoxine 10–25 mg daily** to prevent peripheral neuropathy.  
  - Rifampicin 600 mg once daily (10 mg/kg; max 600 mg)  
    - *If using combination tablet: Rifinah 300 mg (INH) / 150 mg (Rif) – 2 tablets daily for weight ≥ 50 kg; otherwise check BNF.*  
- **Common alternatives:**  
  - Rifampicin monotherapy 600 mg daily for 4 months (if INH contraindicated).  
  - Rifapentine + isoniazid weekly for 3 months (not first-line in UK; used under specialist guidance).  
- **Key contraindications / interactions:**  
  - Rifampicin: strong CYP450 inducer → avoid with warfarin, oral contraceptives (use barrier), HIV protease inhibitors, many others. Check full drug interaction list.  
  - Isoniazid: avoid alcohol (increases hepatotoxicity), monitor LFTs; caution in pre-existing liver disease.  
  - Both: pregnancy – isoniazid + rifampicin can be used (with pyridoxine); rifampicin safe in breastfeeding.  
- **Renal/hepatic adjustment:**  
  - Isoniazid: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; hepatotoxicity monitoring essential.  
  - Rifampicin: no dose adjustment in renal impairment; reduce dose in severe hepatic impairment (check BNF).  
- **Most dangerous dose error:**  
  - **Prescribing isoniazid alone in active TB** – leads to resistance and treatment failure.  
  - **Forgetting pyridoxine** with isoniazid – preventable peripheral neuropathy (especially in malnourished, diabetics, alcoholics, pregnancy).  
  - **Starting rifampicin without checking concurrent medications** – catastrophic drug interactions (e.g. sub-therapeutic warfarin → stroke; reduced OCP efficacy → pregnancy; undetectable antiretroviral levels → HIV treatment failure).

### Complications

- **Acute:**  
  - Drug-induced hepatotoxicity (INH, rifampicin) – LFT elevation, hepatitis (symptoms: nausea, vomiting, jaundice, dark urine). Most common reason for discontinuation. NICE: stop if ALT > 3x ULN with symptoms or > 5x ULN without symptoms.  
  - Hypersensitivity reactions (rash, fever) – both INH and rifampicin.  
  - Thrombocytopenia (rifampicin) – rare; check FBC if bruising/bleeding.  
- **Chronic:**  
  - Peripheral neuropathy (INH) – preventable with pyridoxine.  
  - Optic neuritis (ethambutol – not used in LTBI, but if misclassified).  
  - Iron deficiency (rifampicin – mild, rarely significant).

### Prognosis

- Excellent if treatment completed – reduces risk of reactivation by 60–90% (depending on regimen, adherence, and underlying immune status).  
- Without treatment, 5–10% of immunocompetent patients develop active TB over a lifetime (highest risk in first 2 years).  
- In HIV co-infection, risk of reactivation is 10% per year.

### Pearls (positive actions)

- **Always document “LTBI” vs “active TB” clearly in notes and on the drug chart** – this avoids dangerous monotherapy.  
- **Give pyridoxine with isoniazid** – write “pyridoxine 10 mg daily” as a standing prescription; educate patient why.  
- **Perform a baseline CXR and LFTs before starting LTBI treatment** – and repeat LFTs monthly (or at 2 weeks if risk factors).  
- **Check for drug interactions every time a new drug is added** – rifampicin is a common cause of “unexplained” INR rise or OCP failure.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)

- **Do not treat LTBI without ruling out active TB** – monotherapy will breed resistance and can be fatal.  
- **Do not start LTBI treatment without baseline LFTs** – you cannot attribute hepatotoxicity to drugs if you don’t have a baseline.  
- **Do not assume IGRA positive = LTBI** – false positives occur with NTM, recent BCG (less so), lab error. Always correlate with risk and CXR.  
- **Do not forget to check HIV status** – LTBI treatment is especially important in HIV, but drug interactions with antiretrovirals (especially rifampicin) must be managed.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)

- **Return if:**  
  - New onset fever, cough, night sweats, weight loss – may indicate active TB (or treatment failure).  
  - Symptoms of hepatitis: jaundice, dark urine, pale stools, nausea, vomiting, abdominal pain, unexplained fatigue.  
  - Numbness, tingling, or burning in hands/feet (INH neuropathy) – stop isoniazid, start high-dose pyridoxine 50 mg TDS, and inform TB team.  
- **Follow-up:**  
  - TB clinic review at 1 month, then monthly while on treatment (for LFTs, adherence check, side effects).  
  - After completion: discharge unless ongoing risk (e.g. HIV) – then annual review.

### Escalation Criteria

- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**  
  - LFTs ≥ 3x ULN with symptoms, or ≥ 5x ULN without symptoms – stop drugs, discuss with hepatology/TB team.  
  - Suspected drug-induced rash, urticaria, or anaphylaxis.  
  - Patient develops symptoms of active TB while on LTBI treatment – stop therapy, start 4-drug regimen, isolate.  
- **Call ICU / crash team if:**  
  - Acute liver failure (jaundice, coagulopathy, encephalopathy) – rare but critical.  
  - Anaphylaxis to rifampicin (hypotension, angioedema) – treat with adrenaline, stop drug.  
- **Use NEWS2 thresholds** – LTBI patients should have NEWS2 ≤ 2; if rising, investigate for intercurrent illness or drug reaction.
